topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red 'D-' bloomed starkly on the top of my transcript, a glaring splash of scarlet against the otherwise beige paper. I stared at it, my hand trembling slightly as I traced the ink with a fingertip. It wasn't just a mark; it felt like a verdict, a public declaration of my inadequacy. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a prickly sensation that made me want to physically shrink into my chair. When Professor Albright finally called my name to discuss the failing grade, my voice came out thin and reedy, barely a whisper. "I... I don't understand," I managed, avoiding his gaze entirely. The silence in the lecture hall felt vast, amplifying the sound of my own shallow breathing.

[story 2]

I crumpled the paper into a tight ball, the sound of the tearing paper sharp in the quiet library. It was impossible. I had poured weeks, maybe months, into that final project, believing I had mastered the material. Now, this score suggested a fundamental failure on my part. I walked out of the study room, the weight of the failure pressing down on my shoulders until my chest felt constricted. I kept glancing over my shoulder, half-expecting someone to be watching, to point out the obvious mistake I must have made. When my roommate asked if everything was okay, I just shrugged, a jerky, unnatural movement, and focused intently on tying my shoelace, desperate to escape the suffocating awareness of the poor performance.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my screen, a digital stamp of failure. My stomach plummeted, a cold, heavy drop. I reread the feedback—sparse, clinical, and utterly damning. I closed my laptop lid with a decisive snap that sounded far too loud in the empty dorm room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between my desk and the window. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white. I wanted to scream, to lash out at the unfairness of the grading system, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a dry, rasping sound. I just stood there, breathing in short, shallow gasps, feeling utterly exposed under the harsh glare of my desk lamp.
